content:
3 min read
Just now

--

If you’ve never heard of WSO2 Identity Platform before or previously Asgardeo, you’re probably in the same place I was a few weeks ago building a web app, realizing you need authentication for the pharmacies, and googling and youtubing that "how to add login to my website” literally around 2am. Not even that I search through chatbot platforms that how I’m gonna do it. Cause to be honest it was the day before that we has to launch our platform to our uni supervisors to get feedbacks. and it all the ductions didn’t work we were gonna lose marks in that module. which is Software Development Group Project.
I’m a second-year Software Engineering student and I recently integrated WSO2 Identity Platform into MediFind, a pharmacy locator platform I’m building for Sri Lanka. Here’s what I wish someone had told me before I started.
What it actually is WSO2 Identity Platform
WSO2 Identity Platform is a kind of service that handles user sign-ups, sign-ins, email verifications, and account managements for your app. You don’t build any of that yourself. You redirect your users to WSO2’s login page, they just authenticate for you, and WSO2 sends them back to your app with a token confirming who they are.
That’s it. No building password reset flows. No storing hashed passwords. No email verification system………….. literally WSO2 does all of it.
It’s completely free to start
Go to asgardeo.io, sign up, and you have a working identity provider in minutes. No credit card. The free tier is more than enough for a student project or startup MVP.
The right flow for web apps is PKCE
If you search for OAuth2 tutorials, many still show the “implicit flow” where the access token is returned directly in the URL. That’s deprecated and insecure. The correct approach for single-page apps is Authorization Code flow with PKCE (Proof Key for Code Exchange).
PKCE means your app generates a random secret (the “verifier”), hashes it (the “challenge”), sends the hash to WSO2, and then proves it knows the original secret when exchanging the authorization code for a token. This prevents token interception attacks.
The Web Crypto API makes this straightforward in any modern browser. So no libraries needed.
You can brand the login page
This surprised me for real. In the WSO2 console, you can upload your own logo and set your brand color. So when users are redirected to WSO2’s login page, they see your product’s branding. It feels seamless.
The ID token tells you who the user is
After a successful login, WSO2 gives you an ID token; it’s a JWT (JSON Web Token). You decode it to get the user’s email, name, and other profile info. In JavaScript it’s literally one line:
const user = JSON.parse(atob(idToken.split('.')[1]));
That gives you the user’s email and you can use it however you need in your app.
Start here

• Go to asgardeo.io and create a free account

• Create an organization

• Add a new application then just click on your stack or just click Single Page App

• Set your redirect URL to your app’s URL

• Copy your Client ID

• Implement the PKCE flow in your JavaScript
The whole setup takes under 30 minutes and you’ll have enterprise-grade authentication in your project.
For a student like me, being able to say “I integrated OAuth 2.0 with PKCE using WSO2 Identity Platform” in an interview is a completely different level from “I used Firebase Auth.” You actually understand what’s happening under the hood right……Cheers

I’m Achintha, a Software Engineering undergraduate building MediFind — a healthcare platform for Sri Lanka. Check out the live site at achinthajayaweera.github.io/Medifind-web
